items.summary:
Fix theme updater push and add Photay Appreciation

Use ACTION_GITHUB_TOKEN for checkout so the push can bypass the Vercel
required-status-check on master (enforcement_level: non_admins). The
github-actions bot is a non-admin and has been failing since Apr 25.

Also manually adds the Photay Appreciation theme that was detected on
Apr 25 but never committed due to the push failures.

items.title: Uncle Charlie the Dark Apostle
items.summary: I think I've made a new discovery about an 83-year-old film: Uncle Charlie as an inverted St. Paul, in a Thornton Wilder theological film.

items.title: Shadow of a Doubt
items.summary:
You know what St. Paul said: "Take a little wine for thy stomach's sake." - Uncle Charlie

This second time watching Shadow of a Doubt was the first time I'd noted the two mentions of St. Paul. With Hitchcock, details like this are unlikely to be coincidence, so I decided to pull on that thread. I only had to pull once. The second mention of St. Paul is the aforementioned quote from 1 Timothy 5. This chapter, as it turns out, has a particular interest in widows: "Now she that is a widow indeed, and desolate, trusteth in God, and continueth in supplications and prayers night and day. But she that liveth in pleasure is dead while she liveth."

The first time St. Paul is mentioned is in reference to the Minnesotan city in which Emma and Charlie grew up, the place where, as Emma describes it, Charlie had a terrible bicycle accident that permanently changed him, "He didn't do much reading after that." Paul's conversion occurred after he fell off of his horse.

I don't want to suggest that audiences and critics alike have missed this connection for 80+ years, but I haven't found anyone who's linked Uncle Charlie to St. Paul in this way yet.

animation-appreciation.title: Animation Appreciation
animation-appreciation.description: Michael appreciates animated films and shows for their visual artistry and storytelling, from classic animated films to modern animated series.

items.title: Star Wars: The Mandalorian and Grogu
items.summary:
As an anime enjoyer during my college years, I was well aware of the concept of filler episodes - episodes that were written to fill airtime until more of the manga was out and available to adapt. Key traits of filler were that it needed to be incredibly low stakes and lacking in character development, so as to ensure that the characters would not get out of sync with the source material's plot.

That's what this felt like.

items.title: The Man Who Knew Too Much
items.summary: Watched the earlier version thinking it would enhance this viewing. Instead it left me wishing I was watching that version.

items.title: The Man Who Knew Too Much
items.summary:
I can see why Hitchcock would want to take another run at this. Not just because it feels a bit rough around the edges; it’s also the exact kind of story he loves to tell. I categorize them in my mind as the movies that are about the moral turmoil created by impossible math. Torn Curtain also falls in line with this.

Watching these has helped me identify what I find hollow about Nolan’s films at times. They’re just about the math, often missing the human part.

restraint-in-craft.title: Restraint in Craft
restraint-in-craft.description: Michael appreciates artistic work that achieves impact through subtlety and understatement rather than excess. He values filmmaking, performance, and creative choices that hold back intentionally.
